reason: Both answers reach the same correct core recommendation (dont match; segment defensively; differentiate; lock in contracts; watch the data; expect the competitors pricing to be unsustainable) and both avoid factual errors. They are close in quality, but B is slightly more decision-useful: it quantifies the ARR impact ($3M to about $1.8M) to make the runway risk concrete, gives a more actionable discount policy (a 10-20 percent discretionary band with approval), and adds an operational lever A omits given the explicit 18-month runway constraint: decide within 3 months whether to raise or cut to default-alive. Bs exception condition (churn spikes 2-3x baseline) is also more precise and falsifiable than As vaguer bleeding logos trigger. Both are appropriately concise, so length is not the deciding factor; Bs edge is tighter linkage to the runway constraint and more concrete thresholds.
